trial_data.trial_name: Phase III study of pemetrexed in combination with cisplatin versus cisplatin alone

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus pemetrexed is a preferred first-line regimen for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. This regimen demonstrated improved overall survival compared to cisplatin alone in a phase III trial [133]. An expanded access program showed similar outcomes for cisplatin/pemetrexed and carboplatin/pemetrexed in chemotherapy-naive patients [137].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies of pemetrexed plus carboplatin

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin plus pemetrexed is an alternative preferred first-line regimen for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. Phase II studies reported median overall survival ranging from 12.7 to 14 months [134-136]. An expanded access trial demonstrated similar 1-year survival rates for carboplatin/pemetrexed versus cisplatin/pemetrexed [137].

trial_data.trial_name: Mesothelioma Avastin Cisplatin Pemetrexed Study (MAPS)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus pemetrexed plus bevacizumab is a preferred first-line regimen. The MAPS phase III trial showed improved overall survival with the addition of bevacizumab (HR 0.77; P = .0167) [138]. Bevacizumab is not recommended for patients with uncontrolled hypertension, bleeding/clotting risks, or substantial cardiovascular morbidity. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II study of pemetrexed and carboplatin plus bevacizumab

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin plus pemetrexed plus bevacizumab is an alternative preferred first-line regimen. A phase II study reported a median overall survival of 15.3 months and a partial response rate of 34.2% [139]. Bevacizumab is not recommended for patients with uncontrolled hypertension, bleeding/clotting risks, or substantial cardiovascular morbidity. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 3 randomized controlled trial of pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus pemetrexed plus pembrolizumab is a preferred first-line regimen. A phase 3 trial demonstrated improved overall survival with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (HR 0.79; P = .0324) [150]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing instructions. PD-L1 testing is not required.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase 3 randomized controlled trial of pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy versus chemotherapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin plus pemetrexed plus pembrolizumab is an alternative preferred first-line regimen. A phase 3 trial demonstrated improved overall survival with pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy (HR 0.79; P = .0324) [150]. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab with different dosing instructions. PD-L1 testing is not required.

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 743

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is a preferred first-line regimen for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. The CheckMate 743 phase 3 trial showed improved overall survival with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy (HR 0.75) [147-149]. PD-L1 testing is not required. Immune-related adverse events, such as pneumonitis, may occur; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies of cisplatin and gemcitabine

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus gemcitabine may be useful for patients who cannot receive pemetrexed-based therapy. Partial responses have been reported in phase 2 studies [140-143]. This regimen is considered useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of epithelioid pleural mesothelioma.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II study of gemcitabine combined with carboplatin

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin plus gemcitabine may be useful for patients who cannot receive pemetrexed-based therapy. Partial responses have been reported in a phase 2 study [143]. This regimen is considered useful in certain circumstances for first-line treatment of epithelioid pleural mesothelioma.

trial_data.trial_name: International Expanded Access Program

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent pemetrexed may be used as first-line therapy for patients who are not candidates for platinum-based combination therapy. Results from an International Expanded Access Program support its use [145]. This regimen is considered useful in certain circumstances.

trial_data.trial_name: MS01 trial

tier_1_regimens.notes: Vinorelbine may be used as first-line therapy for patients who are not candidates for platinum-based combination therapy. The MS01 multicentre randomised trial evaluated active symptom control with or without chemotherapy [146]. This regimen is considered useful in certain circumstances.

trial_data.trial_name: CONFIRM

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy is a preferred subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma after progression on first-line chemotherapy. The CONFIRM phase 3 trial demonstrated improved overall survival with nivolumab versus placebo (HR 0.69; P = .009) [157]. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab monotherapy with different dosing instructions.

trial_data.trial_name: IFCT-1501 MAPS2

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is a preferred subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma after progression on first-line chemotherapy. The IFCT-1501 MAPS2 phase 2 trial reported a median overall survival of 15.9 months [158]. High PD-L1 expression (≥25%) was associated with greater overall response in an exploratory analysis. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III study of pemetrexed in combination with cisplatin versus cisplatin alone

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin plus pemetrexed is a preferred subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma after progression on first-line ipilimumab plus nivolumab, if not previously given. Consider rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [166,167].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies of pemetrexed plus carboplatin

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin plus pemetrexed is an alternative preferred subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma after progression on first-line ipilimumab plus nivolumab, if not previously given. Consider rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [166,167].

Consider rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment [166,167]. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for bevacizumab.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase III trial of pemetrexed plus best supportive care versus best supportive care

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent pemetrexed is an other recommended subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. A phase III trial showed no statistically significant difference in overall survival compared to best supportive care alone, but patients could cross over [160]. Consider rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment.

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II study of gemcitabine

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent gemcitabine is an other recommended subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. Phase II studies support its use in pretreated patients [161,20,21].

trial_data.trial_name: RAMES

tier_1_regimens.notes: Gemcitabine plus ramucirumab is an other recommended subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. The RAMES phase 2 trial demonstrated improved overall survival with gemcitabine plus ramucirumab versus gemcitabine plus placebo (HR 0.71; P = .028) [22,162].

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II studies of vinorelbine

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent vinorelbine is an other recommended subsequent therapy for epithelioid pleural mesothelioma. Phase II studies support its use in pretreated patients [23,24].

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 743

tier_1_regimens.notes: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is a preferred first-line regimen for biphasic or sarcomatoid pleural mesothelioma. The CheckMate 743 phase 3 trial showed improved overall survival with nivolumab plus ipilimumab versus chemotherapy (HR 0.75), with a greater benefit in non-epithelioid histology (HR 0.48) [147-149]. PD-L1 testing is not required.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Mesothelioma originates from the cells in the serosal lining (mesothelium) that surrounds the body cavities. Of all mesotheliomas, approximately 85% arise from the pleura, approximately 15% arise from the peritoneum, and the remainder (less than 1%) originate from the pericardium or the tunica vaginalis [1]. Pleural mesothelioma (PM), historically referred to as malignant pleural mesothelioma, is the most common type and affects the pleural space lining the lungs and chest wall [2,4]. In the United States, diffuse pleural mesothelioma affects approximately 3,000 patients each year, with an annual incidence of approximately 1 in 100,000 [2,3]. The purpose of the pathologic evaluation of mesothelioma is based on the pathologic assessment of tumor tissue, which can be obtained from core biopsy sampling, pleurectomy, or other more extensive resections such as extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP). Given its rarity and overlapping microscopic features with other conditions, the histologic diagnosis of diffuse mesothelioma can be challenging [1]. The 2021 World Health Organization (WHO) Classification of Thoracic Tumors introduced new terminology: diffuse pleural mesothelioma (replacing diffuse malignant pleural mesothelioma) and localized pleural mesothelioma (replacing localized malignant pleural mesothelioma) [1,4]. A new entity, mesothelioma in situ, was also added. Almost all mesotheliomas (>99%) are diffuse; however, rare cases of localized pleural mesothelioma have been described, which are solitary, have a different pathogenesis, and harbor a relatively less aggressive clinical course [15-18]. Localized pleural mesothelioma includes three molecular groups (BAP1-mutant, TRAF7-mutant, and near-haploid) with similarities but also differences from diffuse pleural mesothelioma [18]. The BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome is a hereditary cancer syndrome caused by heterozygous germline pathogenic variants in the BAP1 gene [1]. To establish a pathologic diagnosis of mesothelioma, diagnostic tools used clinically include histologic assessment, immunohistochemistry (IHC), cytogenetics, and molecular techniques such as targeted next-generation sequencing (NGS), fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), and single-nucleotide polymorphism arrays [1]. Despite the multiple diagnostic toolkits, the diagnosis relies primarily on proper histologic assessment and IHC.

subtypes.name: Epithelioid Mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Epithelioid mesothelioma is characterized by epithelioid-to-round cells. It is the most common histologic type and has a more favorable prognosis compared with biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [1,96]. Tumor cells are arranged in diverse architectural patterns that include tubulopapillary, trabecular, solid, acinar, micropapillary, or adenomatoid. Rare variants of epithelioid mesothelioma include clear cell, signet ring cell, rhabdoid, deciduoid, and small cell [6-8].
subtypes.frequency: Most common histologic subtype (approximately 60-70% of cases, though exact percentage not specified in source)
key_associations: More favorable prognosis compared to biphasic/sarcomatoid
Higher response rates to platinum/pemetrexed chemotherapy
Surgical resection considered in select patients with clinical stage I disease

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid Mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Sarcomatoid mesothelioma is characterized by spindled cells with tapered nuclei. Subtypes include conventional/spindle cell, desmoplastic, and lymphohistiocytoid [9-13]. A subset exhibits heterologous differentiation with osteosarcomatous, chondrosarcomatous, and/or rhabdomyosarcomatous elements [10].
subtypes.frequency: Less common than epithelioid (approximately 10-15% of cases, exact percentage not specified in source)
key_associations: BAP1 aberrant expression in <20% of cases [38]

subtypes.name: Biphasic (Mixed) Mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Biphasic mesothelioma contains both epithelioid and sarcomatoid components in various proportions, with each comprising at least 10% of the tumor [1]. Assignment of histologic type can be challenging given intertumoral and intratumoral morphologic heterogeneity. Studies comparing concordance between histologic type in initial biopsies with subsequent resections have shown that epithelioid histology in biopsies is not entirely specific and is changed to biphasic or sarcomatoid types in resections in up to 20% of patients [14].
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 20-35% of cases (exact percentage not specified in source)
key_associations: Intermediate prognosis between epithelioid and sarcomatoid
Greater risk of upstaging from epithelioid on biopsy

subtypes.name: Localized Pleural Mesothelioma
subtypes.description: Localized pleural mesothelioma is microscopically identical to diffuse mesothelioma, although it is radiographically and grossly solitary and circumscribed [15-17]. Genetically, it includes three groups (BAP1-mutant, TRAF7-mutant, and near-haploid) with similarities but also differences from diffuse pleural mesothelioma [18].

subtypes.name: Mesothelioma in Situ
subtypes.description: Mesothelioma in situ is a preinvasive, single-layer surface proliferation of neoplastic mesothelial cells. The diagnosis requires either: 1) loss of BAP1 nuclear expression by IHC; and/or 2) CDKN2A homozygous deletion identified either by FISH or by MTAP IHC (cytoplasmic staining). Furthermore, no mass lesions should be identified on imaging or thoracoscopy [1].
subtypes.frequency: New entity in 2021 WHO classification; frequency not specified
key_associations: Requires molecular confirmation (BAP1 IHC or CDKN2A FISH/MTAP IHC)

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Pleural mesothelioma is characterized by recurrent mutations in tumor suppressors and epigenetic regulators. BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein 1) is one of the most frequently altered genes; mechanisms of BAP1 inactivation include point mutations, copy number loss, inactivating structural rearrangements, and minute chromosomal deletions [56-58,61-63]. BAP1 IHC loss (aberrant protein expression, defined as absence of nuclear BAP1 staining) is present in approximately 50% to 70% of epithelioid mesothelioma and in less than 20% of sarcomatoid type [31-37]. BAP1 is a tumor suppressor implicated in the pathogenesis of mesothelioma, uveal melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma, and clear cell renal cell carcinoma [30]. Homozygous loss of CDKN2A (p16) by FISH testing is present in approximately 60% of mesotheliomas [51-53]. Hemizygous loss of NF2 by FISH is present in approximately 50% of pleural mesotheliomas [55]. Alterations in BAP1, NF2, TP53, SETD2, and other genes are identified, affecting multiple pathways in the regulation of cell cycle, RNA processing, histone regulation, and cell growth [56-60]. MTAP (methylthioadenosine phosphorylase) is located near CDKN2A on chromosomal region 9p21. Loss of cytoplasmic MTAP staining is reported in approximately 40% to 60% of mesothelioma but rarely in reactive proliferations [35-37]. Germline mutations are overall present in 12% to 16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesotheliomas and primarily involve genes in the DNA repair and cell cycle regulation, such as BAP1, BRCA2, CDKN2A, TMEM127, VHL, WT1, MRE11A, and MSH6 [42,66,67]. Germline mutations appear to be more common in patients who are young, have a family history of mesothelioma, or have a clinical history of other synchronous malignancies [42,66,68]. A rare subset of pleural mesothelioma harbors a peculiar near-haploid karyotype with extensive loss of heterozygosity involving nearly all chromosomes except chromosomes 5 and 7 [56]. Oncogenic EWSR1::ATF1 fusion has been described in pleural and peritoneal mesotheliomas in young adults [64,65]. ALK rearrangements have been identified in rare patients with peritoneal mesothelioma [43-45,48]. A rare subset harbors unusual genetic alterations including TP53 and/or SETDB1 mutations associated with genomic near-haploidization [56]. Peritoneal mesothelioma has distinct molecular features compared to pleural mesothelioma [64]. Patients with epithelioid mesothelioma with loss of BAP1 by IHC and retained p16 expression by IHC have prolonged survival in both univariate and multivariate analyses [40]. Patients with mesothelioma with germline BAP1 mutations have a prolonged survival [41,42].

epidemiology.incidence: In the United States, diffuse pleural mesothelioma affects approximately 3,000 patients each year, with an annual incidence of approximately 1 in 100,000 [2,3]. Although the incidence has stabilized in the United States in recent years, mesothelioma diagnoses continue to increase worldwide [3].
epidemiology.mortality: Median overall survival (OS) is approximately 1 year after diagnosis of pleural mesothelioma, and 5-year OS is about 5% to 10% [7-14].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence has stabilized in the United States in recent years, but mesothelioma diagnoses continue to increase worldwide [3].
epidemiology.demographics: Most patients diagnosed with pleural mesothelioma are greater than 60 years of age [6,9]. The disease occurs mainly in males who have been exposed to asbestos, although diagnosis may occur decades after exposure [6,7,15,16]. The median latent period is greater than 32 years [15].
epidemiology.anatomic_distribution: Of all mesotheliomas, approximately 85% arise from the pleura, approximately 15% arise from the peritoneum, and the remainder (less than 1%) originate from the pericardium or the tunica vaginalis [1,2,4,5].
epidemiology.histologic_distribution: Epithelioid histology is the most common type and has a more favorable prognosis than biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [9,96]. Most patients present without distant metastases, and central nervous system metastases are rare [71].

risk_factors.detail: Exposure to asbestos is the primary risk factor for pleural mesothelioma. Most mesotheliomas are associated with occupational exposure to asbestos [5,19,20]. The latency period between asbestos exposure and mesothelioma development is long, with a median latent period of greater than 32 years [15]. Some patients exposed to asbestos may only have benign pleural disease [24,25].
Individuals with non-occupational asbestos exposure also have increased risk of mesothelioma [21-23]. Environmental asbestos exposure in certain geographic locations contributes to disease risk.

Patients exposed to ionizing radiation are thought to be at increased risk of developing mesothelioma [20,26-37]. A study evaluating patients from the SEER database found that patients treated with radiation therapy had a higher risk of developing mesothelioma as a secondary malignancy than those who were not irradiated [26].

Erionite is a naturally occurring fiber-like mineral that is more potent than asbestos; however, fewer people are at risk of developing mesothelioma from erionite as it is only found in certain geographic locations and is generally not used for commercial applications [38-42].
risk_factors.strength: Strong risk factor (more potent than asbestos, but limited geographic exposure)

risk_factors.detail: A high incidence of pleural mesothelioma has been reported in families in the absence of exposure to occupational asbestos or erionite; germline mutations in the BAP1 gene were identified as a risk factor [52]. BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome is a hereditary cancer syndrome. Genetic counseling and germline testing may be warranted for patients with a young age at diagnosis, personal or family history suggestive of BAP1 tumor predisposition syndrome, or multi-BAP1-associated malignancies [43-52].

risk_factors.factor: Other germline mutations (DNA repair/cell cycle genes)
risk_factors.detail: Germline mutations in DNA repair and cell cycle regulation genes including BRCA2, CDKN2A, TMEM127, VHL, WT1, MRE11A, and MSH6 have been identified in 12% to 16% of patients with pleural and peritoneal mesotheliomas [42,66,67]. These appear to be more common in patients who are young, have a family history of mesothelioma, or have other synchronous malignancies.

risk_factors.factor: Smoking (note: NOT a risk factor for mesothelioma)
risk_factors.detail: Smoking is not considered a risk factor for mesothelioma [53,54]. However, patients who smoke and have been exposed to asbestos are at increased risk for lung cancer [55,56].
risk_factors.strength: Not a risk factor for mesothelioma specifically

protective_factors: Smoking cessation is recommended as it may negatively affect treatment outcomes, though smoking itself is not a risk factor for mesothelioma development [53-57]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Pleural mesothelioma is a rare malignancy arising from the serosal lining of the pleura, accounting for approximately 85% of all mesotheliomas. It typically affects individuals over 60 years of age with a history of asbestos exposure, often with a latency period of more than 32 years [MS-2]. Patients commonly present with dyspnea and chest wall pain, which are often the most frequent symptoms [MS-3]. Additional symptoms include pleural effusion, fatigue, insomnia, cough, chest wall mass, loss of appetite, and weight loss [MS-3]. Most patients are diagnosed without distant metastases, and central nervous system involvement is rare [MS-3]. The clinical presentation can be insidious, leading to delayed diagnosis due to non-specific symptoms [PM-A]. On physical examination, signs may include decreased breath sounds, dullness to percussion, and palpable chest wall masses. Imaging typically reveals pleural thickening and effusion on chest CT with contrast [PM-1]. The diagnosis requires a multidisciplinary approach, including pathologic confirmation via biopsy, as cytology alone is often insufficient [MS-3].

symptoms.name: Dyspnea
symptoms.detail: Shortness of breath, often progressive and due to pleural effusion or tumor burden. It is a cardinal symptom that may worsen with activity or at rest.
symptoms.frequency: Common; frequently reported in patients with pleural mesothelioma [MS-3].
symptoms.alarm: Persistent or worsening dyspnea in a patient with asbestos exposure should raise suspicion and prompt immediate evaluation.

symptoms.name: Chest wall pain
symptoms.detail: Pain may be localized or diffuse, often related to tumor invasion of chest wall structures or pleural irritation. It can be dull or sharp and may radiate.
symptoms.frequency: Common; one of the hallmark symptoms [MS-3].
symptoms.alarm: Unexplained chest pain, especially if persistent or associated with other symptoms, warrants investigation for malignancy.

symptoms.name: Pleural effusion
symptoms.detail: Accumulation of fluid in the pleural space, causing respiratory symptoms such as dyspnea and cough. Effusions are often exudative and may be recurrent.
symptoms.frequency: Common; often the initial presenting finding on imaging [MS-3].
symptoms.alarm: Recurrent or large pleural effusion in the context of asbestos exposure is concerning and should be evaluated with cytology and biopsy.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Generalized tiredness, may be related to cancer cachexia, anemia, or systemic inflammation.
symptoms.alarm: Unexplained fatigue with other constitutional symptoms should prompt a comprehensive cancer workup.

symptoms.name: Insomnia
symptoms.detail: Difficulty sleeping, potentially due to pain, dyspnea, or anxiety related to the disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common but reported in clinical practice [MS-3].
symptoms.alarm: Insomnia in the context of other symptoms may indicate advanced disease or poor performance status.

symptoms.name: Cough
symptoms.detail: May be dry or productive, related to pleural irritation, compression of airways, or infection.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent cough without clear cause, especially with other red flags, should be evaluated.

symptoms.name: Chest wall mass
symptoms.detail: Palpable mass in advanced cases, indicating local invasion of the chest wall or soft tissues.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; seen in advanced disease [MS-3].
symptoms.alarm: Any palpable mass in the chest wall in a patient with risk factors is alarming and requires urgent biopsy.

symptoms.name: Loss of appetite
symptoms.detail: Anorexia, contributing to weight loss and cachexia.
symptoms.frequency: Common; part of cancer cachexia syndrome [MS-2].
symptoms.alarm: Unintentional appetite loss is a red flag for malignancy and should be assessed.

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unexplained weight loss, often significant, defined as >5% loss over 6 months. It is a poor prognostic indicator.
symptoms.alarm: Weight loss is a classic alarm symptom for cancer and necessitates immediate investigation.

signs.name: Decreased breath sounds
signs.detail: Due to pleural effusion or tumor consolidation, often unilateral.
signs.frequency: Common on physical examination in patients with effusion.

signs.name: Dullness to percussion
signs.detail: Indicative of pleural effusion or solid tumor, typically over the affected hemithorax.

signs.name: Palpable chest wall mass
signs.detail: Sign of local invasion, seen in advanced disease, often tender or fixed.

signs.name: Signs of pleural effusion on imaging
signs.detail: Chest CT often shows pleural thickening, which may be nodular or diffuse, and associated effusion [PM-1].

atypical_or_missed: Presentations with solitary pleural lesions may mimic localized benign disease, such as pleural plaques or fibrosis, leading to misdiagnosis or delayed diagnosis [PM-A].
Symptoms such as fatigue, weight loss, or cough can be attributed to other common conditions like COPD or heart failure, delaying cancer suspicion.
In rare cases, mesothelioma in situ or localized pleural mesothelioma may not show diffuse involvement on imaging, complicating initial detection and requiring high clinical suspicion [PM-A].
Cytologic samples of pleural fluid are often negative or inconclusive even when patients have mesothelioma, which can lead to false reassurance [MS-3].

red_flags: Recurrent or unexplained pleural effusion: In patients with a history of asbestos exposure, this is a key alarm feature that necessitates further investigation, including cytologic assessment and biopsy to rule out mesothelioma [MS-3].
Pleural thickening on chest CT: Irregular, nodular, or diffuse pleural thickening should raise suspicion for mesothelioma and prompt biopsy, as it is a hallmark imaging finding [PM-1].
Unexplained weight loss or appetite loss: Unintentional weight loss of more than 5% over 6 months is a common alarm symptom of malignancy and requires urgent evaluation [MS-2].
Persistent chest pain or dyspnea: Symptoms that are progressive and not explained by other causes, especially in the context of asbestos exposure, warrant immediate diagnostic workup [MS-3].
Performance status 3-4: Patients presenting with poor performance status may have advanced disease and require immediate supportive care assessment and potential palliative interventions [PM-2].
Palpable chest wall mass: Indicates possible local invasion and advanced disease, necessitating urgent staging workup with imaging and biopsy [MS-3].
Rapid symptom progression: Quick worsening of symptoms such as dyspnea or pain may indicate aggressive disease or complications like malignant effusion, requiring urgent intervention [MS-3].

diagnostic.rationale: Initial imaging to evaluate pleural thickening, effusion, and extent of disease; recommended as part of initial evaluation to guide further steps [PM-1].
diagnostic.key_findings: Pleural thickening, which may be nodular or diffuse, pleural effusion, and possible chest wall or mediastinal invasion. CT can also show lymphadenopathy.

diagnostic.rationale: To obtain pleural fluid for cytologic analysis; however, cytology is often negative or inconclusive even in mesothelioma, limiting its diagnostic utility [MS-3].
diagnostic.key_findings: Presence of malignant mesothelial cells may support diagnosis, but negative cytology does not rule out disease, necessitating tissue biopsy.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for histologic confirmation and subtype determination; preferred method is thoracoscopic biopsy to obtain adequate tissue with minimal ports [PM-1]. Other methods include CT-guided core biopsy and open biopsy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic subtype (epithelioid, sarcomatoid, biphasic) and immunohistochemical confirmation of mesothelial origin. Tissue invasion criteria distinguish malignant from reactive proliferations.

diagnostic.rationale: Optional biomarker that may correlate with disease status and burden; not diagnostic but can support clinical assessment in context [MS-3].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated levels may indicate presence of mesothelioma but are not specific, as they can be elevated in other conditions like benign asbestos pleuritis.

staging.test: Chest and abdominal CT with contrast
staging.rationale: Standard staging imaging to assess local extent and abdominal involvement; recommended as part of pretreatment evaluation to guide treatment strategy [PM-2].
staging.indication: All patients with confirmed or suspected pleural mesothelioma.

staging.rationale: For patients being considered for surgery; helps identify metabolically active disease, assess lymph node involvement, and detect distant metastases. Should be performed before pleurodesis to avoid false-positive results from talc inflammation [PM-3].
staging.indication: Clinical stage I epithelioid histology for surgical evaluation.

staging.rationale: To assess mediastinal lymph node involvement; critical for accurate staging in surgical candidates, as N2 disease impacts prognosis and treatment [PM-3].
Optional for further evaluation of chest wall, spinal, diaphragmatic, or vascular involvement based on CT findings; provides better soft tissue contrast [PM-3].
staging.indication: When CT suggests possible invasion of these structures.

staging.rationale: To rule out contralateral or peritoneal disease if suspected, which would indicate stage IV (unresectable) disease and alter management [PM-3].
staging.indication: If imaging suggests transdiaphragmatic extension or peritoneal involvement.

biomarkers.rationale: To confirm mesothelial differentiation and exclude mimics like carcinoma; recommended panel includes at least two mesothelial markers (e.g., calretinin, WT1, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (e.g., claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA) [PM-A].
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes, for pathologic diagnosis to establish mesothelial origin and malignancy.

biomarkers.rationale: Specific marker to distinguish mesothelioma from reactive mesothelial proliferations; loss of nuclear BAP1 expression indicates malignancy and is present in ~50-70% of epithelioid mesothelioma [PM-A].
biomarkers.when: Used in conjunction with other markers for diagnosis, especially in ambiguous cases.
biomarkers.mandatory: Not mandatory but highly specific and recommended.

biomarkers.rationale: Surrogate for CDKN2A loss; loss of cytoplasmic MTAP staining is reported in ~40-60% of mesothelioma and rarely in reactive proliferations, aiding diagnosis [PM-A].
biomarkers.when: When diagnosis is challenging, combined with BAP1 IHC to improve sensitivity and specificity.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but useful in diagnostic workup.

biomarkers.rationale: Homozygous deletion by FISH is present in ~60% of mesotheliomas and supports diagnosis, but it is not specific as it can occur in other tumors [PM-A].
biomarkers.when: In cases of diagnostic uncertainty or when IHC is inconclusive.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but an established ancillary test.

biomarkers.rationale: Recommended to identify rare driver alterations (e.g., NTRK or ALK fusions) for which effective drugs may be available or to counsel patients regarding clinical trials [PM-C].
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis, for all patients with mesothelioma.
biomarkers.mandatory: Recommended but not mandatory; based on clinical judgment.

do_not_miss: Histologic confirmation via adequate biopsy tissue: Cytology alone is often insufficient and may lead to misdiagnosis; thoracoscopic biopsy is preferred to obtain sufficient tissue for subtype and IHC [MS-3].
Immunohistochemical panel to confirm mesothelial origin and exclude other malignancies like carcinoma or sarcoma [PM-A].
Assessment of histologic subtype (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid), as it has significant prognostic value and impacts treatment decisions [PM-A].
avoid: Routine screening with low-dose CT in asymptomatic individuals with asbestos exposure, as there is no evidence that screening improves survival [PM-1 footnote a].
FDG-PET/CT after pleurodesis, as talc produces pleural inflammation leading to false-positive FDG avidity [PM-3].
Surgical resection without prior multidisciplinary evaluation, as the benefit of surgery is unclear and may not improve survival over systemic therapy alone [PM-2 footnote h].
Over-reliance on cytologic diagnosis without tissue biopsy, as it can be negative even in presence of disease [MS-3].

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging System, 9th edition [ST-1, ST-2].

t_stages.description: Tumor limited to the ipsilateral pleura with Psum ≤12 mm and no involvement of the fissure (Fmax ≤5 mm). Psum is the sum of three measurements of maximal pleural thickness along the chest wall or mediastinum in upper, middle, and lower divisions. Fmax is the maximal thickness along fissures on sagittal images [ST-1].

Tumor involving each of the ipsilateral pleura with Psum ≤12 mm and with any of the following: involvement of the fissure (Fmax >5 mm), mediastinal fat invasion, solitary area of chest wall soft tissue invasion; OR tumor involving each of the ipsilateral pleura with Psum >12 mm but ≤30 mm, with or without involvement of the fissure, mediastinal fat invasion, or solitary area of chest wall soft tissue invasion [ST-1].
Tumor involving the ipsilateral pleura with Psum >30 mm, with or without involvement of the fissure (Fmax >5 mm), mediastinal fat invasion, or solitary area of chest wall soft tissue invasion [ST-1].

Tumor with invasion of any of the following: chest wall bony invasion (rib), mediastinal organs (heart, spine, esophagus, trachea, great vessels), diffuse chest wall invasion, direct tumor extension through the diaphragm or pericardium, direct tumor extension to the contralateral pleura, presence of malignant pericardial effusion [ST-1].

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
No tumor involvement of regional lymph node(s).

Tumor involvement of ipsilateral bronchopulmonary, hilar, or mediastinal (including the internal mammary, peridiaphragmatic, pericardial fat pad, or intercostal lymph nodes) regional lymph nodes [ST-2].
Tumor involvement of contralateral mediastinal, ipsilateral, or contralateral supraclavicular lymph nodes [ST-2].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to ipsilateral pleura without lymph node involvement or distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated in the source; however, overall median survival for pleural mesothelioma is approximately 1 year, with 5-year survival about 5-10% [MS-2].
stage_groups.treatment_intent: For clinical stage I with epithelioid histology, systemic therapy is preferred, but surgical evaluation may be considered in select patients [PM-2].

stage_groups.criteria: T2 N0 M0 or T1 N1 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with limited extension or ipsilateral lymph node involvement, but no distant metastasis.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified; survival is poor with median OS around 1 year.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy is recommended; surgery is not recommended for stage II-IV regardless of histology [PM-2].

stage_groups.criteria: T2 N1 M0 or T3 N0-N1 M0 or T1-T3 N2 M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced tumor with possible mediastinal lymph node involvement, indicating more extensive disease.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not specified; survival is diminished with N2 disease.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Systemic therapy; surgery may be considered only in clinical trials or at expert centers for N2 disease [PM-2].

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor with invasion of adjacent structures, considered unresectable.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Poor; median OS likely less than 1 year.
Very poor; median OS likely less than 1 year.

staging_pearls: Staging is based on both clinical and pathological assessment; clinical staging uses imaging, while pathological staging is determined after surgical resection [ST-1, ST-2].
Tumor thickness is measured using Psum (sum of three maximal pleural thickness measurements) and Fmax (maximal fissure thickness), which are specific to mesothelioma staging [ST-1].
N2 disease is associated with substantially diminished prognosis, and surgical resection should only be considered in clinical trials or at centers with expertise in mesothelioma [PM-2].
The 9th edition AJCC staging system incorporates specific measurements for pleural tumor extent, emphasizing the importance of accurate imaging and pathologic evaluation [ST-1].
Epithelioid histology generally has a better prognosis than biphasic or sarcomatoid histology, but staging is uniform across histologic types [PM-A].

management_principles.overview: Pleural mesothelioma (PM) is a rare cancer originating from mesothelial surfaces of the pleura, estimated to occur in approximately 3,000 people in the United States each year.1,2 PM accounts for approximately 85% of all mesotheliomas; approximately 15% arise from the peritoneum, and less than 1% from the pericardium or tunica vaginalis testis.2,4,5 Treatment philosophy centers on systemic therapy as the primary treatment modality for most patients with PM, regardless of resectability. The median overall survival (OS) is approximately 1 year after diagnosis, and 5-year OS is approximately 5% to 10%.7-14 Most patients diagnosed with PM are over 60 years of age.6,9 The disease occurs mainly in males with asbestos exposure, with a median latent period exceeding 32 years.15,16 Two immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI)-containing regimens have demonstrated efficacy as first-line therapy: nivolumab plus ipilimumab (category 1) and pembrolizumab plus platinum/pemetrexed chemotherapy (category 1).9,8 Surgery is no longer considered a routine primary treatment for most patients, based on randomized trial data from MARS and MARS2 demonstrating that cytoreductive surgery does not improve survival compared with systemic therapy alone.187,188 Treatment decisions require a multidisciplinary approach integrating clinical stage, histologic subtype, performance status (PS), and patient preference. All regimens recommended for PM can also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma.155,156 Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify rare driver alterations such as NTRK or ALK fusions.25

by_intent.patient_group: Select patients with clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology, PS 0-2, who are medically operable
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy as primary treatment with consideration of surgical evaluation for carefully selected patients; pleural IMRT may be considered sequentially. If surgery is pursued after multidisciplinary evaluation, P/D is recommended over EPP. The benefit of surgical resection is unclear and there is no evidence that patient survival is improved with surgery combined with systemic therapy versus systemic therapy alone.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with clinical stage II-IV PM with epithelioid histology; sarcomatoid or biphasic histology at any stage; medically inoperable patients at any stage; PS 0-2
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy as primary treatment modality. Observation may be considered for asymptomatic patients with minimal disease burden if systemic therapy is planned at progression. RT is an effective palliative treatment for chest pain, bronchial or esophageal obstruction, or other symptomatic sites.
Best supportive care is recommended. RT and image-guided thermal ablation are palliative options for symptomatic pleural disease.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management by a multidisciplinary pleural team with experience in pleural mesothelioma is recommended. The multidisciplinary team should include diagnostic imaging specialists, pulmonologists, surgeons, medical oncologists, and radiation oncologists. Recommendations regarding RT should be made by radiation oncologists with experience in managing pleural mesothelioma. The best timing for delivering RT after surgical intervention and/or in conjunction with chemotherapy should be discussed by a multidisciplinary team. Surgical resection should be performed on carefully evaluated patients by thoracic surgeons with experience in managing pleural mesothelioma.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Patients with PS 0-2 are candidates for active systemic therapy with or without consideration of surgical evaluation (for stage I epithelioid). Patients with PS 3-4 should receive best supportive care. The NCCN Panel recommends best supportive care for patients presenting with PS 3-4.

management_pathways.title: Clinical Stage I PM with Epithelioid Histology - Surgical Evaluation and Treatment

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology, PS 0-2, medically operable

options.label: Systemic therapy (preferred primary treatment)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is the preferred primary treatment for clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology. Pleural IMRT may be considered sequentially.

options.label: Surgical evaluation for potential resection
options.recommendation: Surgical evaluation should be considered only for select patients. The benefit of surgical resection is unclear. If surgery is deemed viable, P/D is recommended over EPP. FDG-PET/CT and invasive mediastinal staging required before surgery.

adjuvant_logic.summary: If surgery is pursued, systemic therapy can be given as either induction or adjuvant therapy. Sequential pleural IMRT at a center with experience may be considered after surgery and/or systemic therapy.

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology, PS 0-2, asymptomatic with minimal disease burden

options.label: Observation for progression
options.recommendation: Observation may be considered if systemic therapy is planned at the time of symptomatic or radiographic progression.

management_pathways.title: Clinical Stage I PM with Epithelioid Histology - Surgical Treatment Pathway

options.label: Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D)
options.recommendation: P/D is the preferred surgical treatment option. Consider sequential pleural IMRT after P/D.

options.label: Sequential pleural IMRT
options.recommendation: Consider sequential pleural IMRT at centers with experience. Should only be performed in carefully selected patients at centers with experience.

adjuvant_logic.summary: After recovery from surgery, patients should be referred for adjuvant therapy, which may include chemotherapy and RT.

options.label: Systemic therapy and consider sequential pleural IMRT
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy and consider sequential pleural IMRT.

management_pathways.title: Clinical Stage II-IV PM with Epithelioid Histology; Sarcomatoid or Biphasic Histology (Any Stage); or Medically Inoperable

nodes.criteria: Clinical stage II-IV with epithelioid histology OR sarcomatoid/biphasic histology at any stage OR medically inoperable any stage; PS 0-2

options.label: First-line systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is the primary treatment. ICI-based regimens are favored for non-epithelioid histology.

nodes.criteria: Asymptomatic with minimal disease burden, PS 0-2

options.label: Observation for progression
options.recommendation: Observation may be considered for patients who are not eligible for surgery and are asymptomatic with minimal burden of disease if systemic therapy is planned when progression occurs.

management_pathways.title: Disease Progression After First-Line Chemotherapy (No Prior Immunotherapy)

nodes.criteria: Progression after first-line chemotherapy without prior immunotherapy

options.label: Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Nivolumab monotherapy is preferred subsequent therapy. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV nivolumab.

options.label: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Ipilimumab plus nivolumab is preferred subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: Progression after first-line chemotherapy; good prior response to pemetrexed

options.label: Rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy
options.recommendation: Rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy may be considered if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment.

options.label: Platinum/pemetrexed (with or without bevacizumab)
options.recommendation: Carboplatin or cisplatin in combination with pemetrexed (with or without bevacizumab) are appropriate options if not given previously.

options.label: Pemetrexed (category 1)
options.recommendation: Single-agent pemetrexed is other recommended subsequent therapy (category 1).

options.label: Gemcitabine
options.recommendation: Single-agent gemcitabine is other recommended subsequent therapy.

options.label: Gemcitabine + Ramucirumab
options.recommendation: Gemcitabine plus ramucirumab is other recommended subsequent therapy.

options.label: Vinorelbine
options.recommendation: Single-agent vinorelbine is other recommended subsequent therapy.

nodes.criteria: PS 3-4 regardless of stage or histology

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care is recommended for patients presenting with PS 3-4. RT and image-guided thermal ablation are palliative options for symptomatic pleural disease.

items.recommendation: Required as part of initial and pretreatment evaluation for staging and to guide treatment strategy

items.text: Chest and abdominal CT with contrast
items.recommendation: Recommended as part of pretreatment evaluation
Recommended for patients being considered for surgery; should be obtained before pleurodesis if practical, as talc produces pleural inflammation which can affect FDG avidity (false-positive results)
Optional, for further evaluation of possible chest, spinal, diaphragmatic, or vascular involvement based on CT imaging

items.text: Pleural biopsy (thoracoscopic biopsy [preferred], CT-guided core biopsy, open biopsy, Abrams needle)
items.recommendation: Required to confirm diagnosis. Thoracoscopy preferred. Cytologic samples are generally not considered adequate for important histologic differentiation required for treatment decisions
Part of initial evaluation; cytology is often negative even when patients have PM

Essential for diagnosis. Panel including at least two mesothelial markers (eg, calretinin, WT1, D2-40) and two carcinoma markers (eg, claudin-4, TTF-1, polyclonal CEA) recommended. BAP1 IHC, MTAP IHC, and CDKN2A (p16) FISH are established markers for diagnosing mesothelioma

items.text: Determination of histologic subtype (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid)
items.recommendation: Required for prognostication and treatment planning. Accuracy of typing increases with higher number of biopsies
Recommended with the goal of identifying rare driver alterations (eg, NTRK or ALK) for which effective drugs may be available or to counsel patients regarding clinical trials
Optional; levels may correlate with disease status

items.text: Mediastinoscopy or endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS)/endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of mediastinal lymph nodes
items.recommendation: Recommended for patients being considered for surgery

items.text: Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) including diffusing capacity of the lung for carbon monoxide (DLCO)
items.recommendation: Required for patients being considered for surgery to assess operability
Required for patients being considered for surgery
Consider if suspicion of contralateral or peritoneal disease based on imaging studies

items.text: Assessment for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List
items.recommendation: Recommended at initial evaluation; includes social determinants of health

items.text: Symptom assessment (dyspnea, chest wall pain, fatigue, cough, weight loss)
items.recommendation: PM patients may have high symptom burden compared to other cancer types

surgery.role: Surgery is no longer considered a routine primary treatment option for most patients with PM based on data from MARS and MARS2 randomized trials. Systemic therapy is now the preferred primary treatment for most patients. However, surgical evaluation may be considered for select patients with clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology and PS 0-2 who are medically operable.
principles: Surgical resection should be performed on carefully evaluated patients by thoracic surgeons with experience in managing pleural mesothelioma
Decisions regarding surgical options are highly dependent on accurate histology; cytology is generally not considered adequate for histologic differentiation required for treatment decisions
For patients being considered for surgery, a single-port thoracoscopy on the line of the potential incision is recommended
The goal of surgery is complete gross cytoreduction of the tumor (macroscopic complete resection)
If macroscopic complete resection is not possible (eg, multiple sites of chest wall invasion), surgery should be aborted
If most of gross disease can be removed to help with postoperative management with minimal impact on morbidity, surgery should be continued
Mediastinal nodal sampling should be performed with a goal to obtain at least 3 nodal stations
P/D is the preferred surgical treatment option over EPP
If N2 disease is identified, prognosis is substantially diminished; surgical resection should only be considered in clinical trials or at expert centers

procedures.name: Pleurectomy/Decortication (P/D)
procedures.approach: Lung-sparing; complete removal of the pleura and all gross tumor, with or without en-bloc resection of pericardium and/or diaphragm with reconstruction. Extended P/D includes resection of diaphragm and pericardium in addition to total pleurectomy.
procedures.indications: Clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology in select patients after careful multidisciplinary evaluation; preferred surgical option when surgery is pursued
procedures.key_complications: Postoperative complications; less morbidity and mortality compared to EPP

procedures.name: Extrapleural Pneumonectomy (EPP)
procedures.approach: En-bloc resection of the pleura, lung, ipsilateral diaphragm, and often the pericardium
procedures.indications: Rare circumstances requiring careful consideration of the total treatment plan by the patient and multidisciplinary team
procedures.key_complications: Higher morbidity and mortality than P/D; 12-month survival rate was 52.2% in the MARS trial; perioperative deaths occurred in 12.5% (3/24) of patients

surgery.resectability_criteria: Surgical evaluation should be considered only for select patients with clinical stage I PM with epithelioid histology who are medically operable and can tolerate the risks associated with surgery. The benefit of surgical resection in PM is unclear and there is no evidence that patient survival is improved with surgery combined with systemic therapy versus systemic therapy alone. Cytoreductive surgery is not recommended as a primary treatment option for patients with stage II to IV PM regardless of histology. Surgery is not recommended for patients with sarcomatoid or biphasic histology as it is associated with worse survival outcomes. If N2 disease is identified, surgical resection should only be considered in clinical trials or at expert centers.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: FDG-PET/CT should be obtained before pleurodesis if practical. Invasive mediastinal staging (mediastinoscopy, EBUS/EUS FNA) is required. PFTs including DLCO and cardiac stress tests are required to assess operability. Chest MRI with contrast may be considered for evaluation of chest wall, spinal, diaphragmatic, or vascular involvement.

radiation_therapy.role: RT plays a supportive role in the treatment of PM. It is not routinely used as primary treatment but can be considered as sequential therapy after systemic therapy and/or P/D in select patients with stage I epithelioid histology. RT is an effective palliative treatment for relief of chest pain, bronchial or esophageal obstruction, or other symptomatic sites. Prophylactic RT is not routinely recommended to prevent instrument-tract recurrence.
principles: Recommendations regarding RT should be made by radiation oncologists with experience in managing pleural mesothelioma
The best timing for delivering RT after surgical intervention and/or in conjunction with chemotherapy should be discussed by a multidisciplinary team
PET scanning for treatment planning can be used as indicated
Prophylactic RT is not routinely recommended to prevent instrument-tract recurrence after pleural intervention
Pleural IMRT after P/D in the presence of an intact lung may be considered in centers with experience, given the technical difficulty of this treatment
Advanced technologies may be used such as IGRT, SRS, SBRT, and IMPT
Special attention should be paid to minimize radiation to the contralateral lung as the risk of fatal pneumonitis with IMRT is excessively high when strict limits are not applied
Extensive elective nodal irradiation (ENI) is not recommended

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative - Chest wall pain from recurrent nodules
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliation of chest wall pain from recurrent pleural nodules

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative - Multiple brain or bone metastases
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliation of multiple brain or bone metastases

dose_frameworks.name: Post P/D - Higher dose to higher risk areas
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant RT after pleurectomy/decortication; higher dose to higher risk areas

approaches.name: Pleural Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be used in conjunction with systemic therapy; timing discussed by multidisciplinary team
approaches.indication: Sequential therapy after systemic therapy and/or P/D for select patients with stage I PM with epithelioid histology at centers with experience
approaches.key_trial: Phase 3 randomized trial (Trovo et al.) demonstrated substantially greater overall survival with sequential pleural IMRT compared to palliative RT (median OS 25.6 vs 12.4 months, P<.001; 2-year OS 58% vs 28%, HR 0.54, P=.031). IMPRINT phase 2 trial (Rimner et al.) evaluated safety with 30% grade >=2 radiation pneumonitis.
approaches.toxicities: Grade >=2 radiation pneumonitis in 30% of patients; fatal pneumonitis risk if strict lung dose limits not maintained

approaches.name: Palliative Radiation Therapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: 20-40 Gy in fractions of >=4 Gy; or 30 Gy in 3 Gy fractions
approaches.indication: Palliative treatment for chest pain, bronchial or esophageal obstruction, or other symptomatic sites
approaches.key_trial: Daily doses of 4 Gy appear more efficacious than fractions of <4 Gy for chest pain relief

approaches.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)/Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS)
approaches.indication: Oligometastatic disease (brain, bone); may be considered for oligorecurrent pleural disease

radiation_therapy.contraindications_warnings: Prophylactic RT is not routinely recommended to prevent instrument-tract recurrence after pleural intervention. The SMART trial showed no significant difference in procedure-tract recurrence between prophylactic RT (9%) and deferred RT (16%) (OR 0.51, P=.14). Fatal pneumonitis may occur with IMRT if strict limits are not applied to the contralateral lung. The contralateral uninvolved mean lung dose should be kept <8.5 Gy. For postoperative RT after P/D: limit ipsilateral lung dose, keep total MLD <21 Gy, V20 <40%, contralateral lung V20 <7% and MLD <8 Gy.
radiation_therapy.volume_guidelines: GTV should include any grossly visible tumor and surgical clips. CTV for adjuvant RT after P/D should encompass the entire pleural surface, surgical clips, and potential sites with residual disease. PTV should consider target motion and daily setup errors. ENI (entire mediastinum and bilateral supraclavicular nodal regions) is not recommended.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is the primary treatment modality for most patients with PM. Historically, cytotoxic chemotherapy was the mainstay, but two ICI-containing regimens have now demonstrated efficacy in the first-line setting and are FDA approved. All regimens can also be used for pericardial mesothelioma and tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma. Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify rare driver alterations (eg, NTRK, ALK). For patients with non-epithelioid histology, ICI-based therapy is favored as non-epithelioid PM appears less chemosensitive than epithelioid PM. PD-L1 testing is not required for prescribing ICI-containing regimens.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (category 1) OR (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Pembrolizumab (category 1) OR (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed (category 1) OR (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Nivolumab plus ipilimumab: CheckMate 743 phase 3 trial showed median OS 18.1 months vs 14.1 months with chemotherapy (HR 0.75) at 5-year follow-up. Among epithelioid subgroup: HR 0.85. Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy: phase 3 trial showed median OS 17.3 vs 16.1 months (HR 0.79, P=.0324). Platinum/pemetrexed: FDA approved based on phase 3 trial showing median OS 12.1 vs 9.3 months (P=.02). Bevacizumab addition: MAPS trial showed median OS 18.8 vs 16.1 months (HR 0.77, P=.0167).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing not required. Broad molecular profiling recommended for rare driver alterations.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (category 1) OR (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Pembrolizumab (category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Non-epithelioid histology shows greater benefit from ICI-based regimens. In CheckMate 743, non-epithelioid subgroup: median OS 18.1 vs 8.8 months with chemotherapy (HR 0.48).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent Therapy - After First-Line Chemotherapy (No Prior ICI)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CONFIRM phase 3 trial: nivolumab median OS 10.2 vs 6.9 months with placebo (HR 0.69, P=.009). MAPS2 phase 2 trial: 12-week DCR 40% (ipi/nivo) and 52% (nivo alone); median OS 15.9 months (ipi/nivo) and 11.9 months (nivo alone).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed with or without Bevacizumab
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Strength of data supporting platinum-based chemotherapy regimens in the first-line setting supports use in subsequent lines if not previously given.

Rechallenge may be effective if good disease response to first-line pemetrexed-based treatment. Retrospective data showed rechallenging with pemetrexed/platinum reduced risk of death vs pemetrexed alone (HR 0.11, P<.001).

systemic_therapy.contraindications: Bevacizumab is not recommended for patients with uncontrolled hypertension, risk for bleeding or clotting, and substantial cardiovascular morbidity. Pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab but has different dosing and administration instructions. Nivolumab and hyaluronidase-nvhy is not approved for concurrent use with IV ipilimumab; however, for nivolumab monotherapy, the subcutaneous formulation may be substituted. Immune-related AEs, such as pneumonitis, may occur with ICI treatment.

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line (all histologies, preferred for non-epithelioid); Subsequent (after chemo)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs in 31% (CheckMate 743). Immune-related AEs including pneumonitis.

key_regimens.name: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs in 27%. Pemetrexed requires folic acid and B12 supplementation.

key_regimens.name: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed
key_regimens.key_toxicities: FDA approved; phase 3 trial showed median OS 12.1 vs 9.3 months (P=.02). Folic acid and B12 supplementation required with pemetrexed.

key_regimens.name: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Pemetrexed + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: MAPS trial: Grade 3-4 AEs in 71% vs 62% without bevacizumab. More grade 3+ hypertension (23% vs 0%), proteinuria (3% vs 0%), and thrombotic events (6% vs 1%). Not recommended for patients with uncontrolled hypertension, bleeding/clotting risk, or substantial cardiovascular morbidity.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: CONFIRM trial: median OS 10.2 vs 6.9 months with placebo (HR 0.69, P=.009). Diarrhea and infusion-related reactions most common grade 3+ AEs. Serious AEs similar to placebo (41% vs 44%).

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine + Ramucirumab
key_regimens.key_toxicities: RAMES trial: median OS 13.8 vs 7.5 months (HR 0.71, P=.028). Grade 3-4 treatment-related AEs in 44% vs 30%.

key_regimens.name: (Carboplatin or Cisplatin)/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Useful for patients who cannot receive pemetrexed. Partial responses reported in phase 2 studies.

key_regimens.name: Pemetrexed
key_regimens.key_toxicities: For patients who are not candidates for platinum-based combination therapy. Rechallenge can be considered if good prior response.

key_regimens.name: Vinorelbine
key_regimens.key_toxicities: For patients who are not candidates for platinum-based combination therapy.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment for Pleural Mesothelioma
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment should be performed periodically during and after systemic therapy. The specific interval depends on the treatment regimen and clinical context.
response_logic: Response to first-line systemic therapy should be assessed with imaging (CT chest/abdomen with contrast) at regular intervals
Progression is defined by radiographic or symptomatic progression
Upon disease progression, subsequent therapy should be initiated based on the type of first-line treatment received
If chemotherapy was first-line, nivolumab with or without ipilimumab is preferred subsequent therapy
If ipilimumab/nivolumab was first-line, platinum/pemetrexed (with or without bevacizumab) is preferred subsequent therapy
Rechallenge with pemetrexed-based therapy may be considered if good response to front-line pemetrexed-based treatment
imaging_recommendations: Chest CT with contrast for response assessment and surveillance
Chest and abdominal CT with contrast as part of staging
FDG-PET/CT may be used for staging and treatment planning; should be obtained before pleurodesis
Chest MRI with contrast optional for evaluating local extent of disease
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Tissue confirmation of progression is not routinely required before initiating subsequent therapy
Mediastinoscopy or EBUS/EUS may be performed for restaging if clinically indicated
For patients with progression on systemic therapy, options depend on prior treatment: ICI-based therapy if prior chemo-only; platinum/pemetrexed-based therapy if prior ICI-based therapy
Palliative RT or image-guided thermal ablation may be considered for symptomatic progressive pleural disease

prognosis.overall: Pleural mesothelioma (PM) is a rare, aggressive cancer with a generally poor prognosis. Median overall survival (OS) after diagnosis is approximately 1 year, and the 5-year OS rate is about 5% to 10% [MS-3, 7-14]. The prognosis is heavily influenced by histologic subtype, with epithelioid histology conferring a more favorable prognosis than biphasic or sarcomatoid histologies [9,96]. Most patients present with advanced disease, and CNS metastases are rare [71]. Incidence is estimated at ~3,000 cases annually in the United States, with a latent period from asbestos exposure typically >32 years [15,16].

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided as a standalone figure in the source.
by_stage.context: Clinical stage I disease with epithelioid histology may be considered for surgical evaluation, but the benefit of surgical resection is unclear, and survival is not proven to be improved with surgery plus systemic therapy versus systemic therapy alone [MS-2]. The 5-year survival for all stages combined is 5%-10%.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly provided as standalone figures.
by_stage.context: Systemic therapy is the primary treatment modality for most patients with stage II-IV disease regardless of histology, or for those with sarcomatoid/biphasic histology at any stage. Prognosis with N2 disease is substantially diminished, and surgical resection is only considered in specialized centers or trials [MS-10, MS-13].

prognostic_factors: Histologic subtype (epithelioid is more favorable than biphasic or sarcomatoid) [9,96].
Clinical stage (earlier stage is more favorable) [ST-2].
Performance status (PS 0-2 is required for active treatment; PS 3-4 indicates best supportive care) [MS-2, MS-14].

BAP1 expression and mutations: Patients with epithelioid mesothelioma and loss of BAP1 by IHC with retained p16 expression have prolonged survival [40]. Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have improved survival [41,42].
PD-L1 expression: Its utility as a predictive biomarker for immunotherapy in mesothelioma remains unclear [49, MS-7].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis is poor overall, but varies significantly based on histology and stage. While systemic therapy has improved outcomes, long-term survival remains uncommon. Genetic factors like BAP1 mutations are associated with better survival outcomes [40-42]. Treatment is guided by histology and clinical stage, with systemic therapy being the mainstay for most patients.

disease_related.complication: Recurrent pleural effusion causing dyspnea and chest discomfort.
disease_related.management: Talc pleurodesis or placement of an indwelling pleural catheter for management. Drainage is preferred for potentially operable candidates; either drainage or pleurodesis is an option for inoperable patients [PM-B]. Therapeutic/palliative thoracentesis can also be used.

disease_related.complication: Chest wall pain from recurrent nodules or pleural disease.
disease_related.management: Palliative radiation therapy. Daily doses of ≥4 Gy appear more efficacious for pain relief than lower fractions, though the optimal regimen is unclear [MS-11, PM-D].

chemotherapy: Hematologic toxicities (neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia) are common with platinum/pemetrexed regimens [1,2,3,4].
Fatigue, nausea/vomiting, and mucositis are typical side effects managed per NCCN guidelines [PM-B].
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) such as pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, and skin reactions can occur with nivolumab, ipilimumab, or pembrolizumab. Management follows the NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [MS-7, PM-B].
targeted_therapy: Bevacizumab is associated with increased rates of grade 3-4 hypertension (23% vs. 0%), proteinuria (3% vs. 0%), and thrombotic events (6% vs. 1%) when added to chemotherapy. It is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension, bleeding/clotting risk, or significant cardiovascular morbidity [MS-5].
surgery: Pleurectomy/decortication (P/D) and extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP) carry significant perioperative morbidity and mortality. The MARS trial noted a perioperative mortality of 12.5% for EPP [187].
Lung-sparing P/D is associated with lower perioperative mortality than EPP but still carries risks [MS-9, MS-10].
radiation: Radiation pneumonitis is a significant risk, especially with pleural IMRT. Fatal pneumonitis can occur if strict dose limits to the contralateral lung are not applied [13, MS-11].
The contralateral mean lung dose should be kept <8.5 Gy, and contralateral V20 <7% [15, MS-11, PM-D].
Other toxicities include esophagitis, fatigue, and skin reactions.

supportive_care.overview: Principles of supportive care and survivorship are integrated into the management of pleural mesothelioma to manage symptoms, treatment side effects, and improve quality of life. This includes management of pleural effusions, pain, nausea, distress, fatigue, immunotherapy toxicities, venous thromboembolic disease, and survivorship care [PM-B].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Referenced indirectly through general cancer supportive care principles; specific nutritional guidance for mesothelioma is not detailed in the source. Consultation with a dietitian is typically recommended.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Per NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis [PM-B]. Regimen-dependent; highly emetogenic chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin) requires aggressive prophylaxis.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not explicitly detailed in the provided source. General oncology principles for neutropenia risk reduction with myelosuppressive chemotherapy apply.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolic Disease [PM-B]. Patients with mesothelioma are at risk, and vigilance for VTE symptoms is required.
supportive_care.pain_management: Follow the NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Options include radiation for chest wall pain, interventional procedures, and systemic analgesics [PM-B, MS-11].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Assess for distress at initial evaluation using tools like the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List. Refer to the NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management [PM-1, PM-B]. Address social determinants of health.
supportive_care.dental_care: Not specified in the provided source. General pre-treatment dental evaluation is recommended for patients receiving chemotherapy or radiation to the head/neck.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For the rare patients who undergo what is considered curative-intent multimodality therapy (e.g., systemic therapy + surgery ± RT for stage I epithelioid), close follow-up is warranted to detect recurrence. No specific standardized post-curative schedule is provided in the NCCN document.
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The primary rationale for surveillance is to detect disease progression or recurrence early to guide subsequent therapy, manage symptoms, and evaluate eligibility for clinical trials. Surveillance also monitors for long-term effects of treatment.
late_effects_screening: Screening for treatment-related toxicities: pneumonitis (after IMRT or immunotherapy), renal dysfunction (after cisplatin), cardiac toxicity (after radiation).

Monitoring for second malignancies, particularly in patients with germline BAP1 mutations who have an increased risk of other cancers (e.g., uveal melanoma, cutaneous melanoma, renal cell carcinoma) [51].
Assessment of respiratory function and quality of life.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences are local-regional within the pleural cavity. Distant metastases can occur but are less common. CNS metastases are rare [71]. Pattern of failure after surgery and adjuvant IMRT may include mediastinal nodal failure (22%) and distant progression (48%) [195].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: No explicit, standardized follow-up schedule is provided in the NCCN Guidelines for pleural mesothelioma. Follow-up frequency and intensity are determined by the treating multidisciplinary team based on the treatment received, disease status, and patient factors.
imaging_strategy: Chest/abdomen CT with contrast is used for initial staging [MS-5].
FDG-PET/CT may be used for initial staging and to assess metabolic activity, ideally before pleurodesis [MS-5].
Response assessment and surveillance typically involve periodic CT scans, though the exact interval is not mandated.
laboratory_monitoring: Soluble mesothelin-related peptide (SMRP) is an optional biomarker that may correlate with disease status and be used for monitoring [PM-1, MS-3].
Complete blood counts and metabolic panels are monitored during systemic therapy to assess toxicity.
supportive_follow_up: Ongoing assessment and management of symptoms (dyspnea, pain).
Reassessment of nutritional status and psychosocial distress.
Monitoring for and managing long-term sequelae of treatment (e.g., pulmonary fibrosis, cardiac issues).

special_situations.key_message: All systemic therapy regimens recommended for pleural mesothelioma can also be used for pericardial mesothelioma.
special_situations.management: Extrapolated from pleural mesothelioma treatment. Systemic therapy is the primary approach.
special_situations.evidence: Category 2A recommendation based on extrapolation from pleural mesothelioma data due to rarity [PM-C].

special_situations.key_message: All systemic therapy regimens recommended for pleural mesothelioma can also be used for tunica vaginalis testis mesothelioma.
special_situations.management: Systemic therapy per pleural mesothelioma guidelines. Surgical management (radical orchiectomy) is part of primary treatment.

special_situations.key_message: Localized pleural mesothelioma is a rare (<1%), solitary, circumscribed lesion with a less aggressive clinical course than diffuse mesothelioma.
special_situations.management: Surgical resection is the primary treatment approach. Molecularly, it can be divided into BAP1-mutant, TRAF7-mutant, and near-haploid groups [PM-A].
special_situations.evidence: Recognized as a distinct entity in the 2021 WHO Classification [1, PM-A].

special_situations.key_message: A preinvasive, single-layer surface proliferation of neoplastic mesothelial cells without a mass lesion on imaging.
special_situations.management: Diagnosis requires loss of BAP1 nuclear expression by IHC and/or CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Management may include surveillance or resection.
special_situations.evidence: New entity in the 2021 WHO Classification [1, PM-A].

special_situations.key_message: Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have improved survival and are at risk for multiple BAP1-associated malignancies.
special_situations.management: Genetic counseling and germline testing may be warranted, especially with young age, family history, or multiple primary tumors. Surveillance for other cancers is recommended.
special_situations.evidence: Germline mutations occur in 12%-16% of patients and are associated with prolonged survival [41,42,47, MS-3].

special_situations.key_message: A rare subset, particularly in younger patients and children, with dramatic response to ALK inhibitors.
special_situations.management: Broad molecular tumor profiling is recommended to identify such rare drivers. ALK inhibitors (e.g., ceritinib) are effective.
special_situations.evidence: Case reports and series demonstrate dramatic responses [43-48].

clinical_pearls: Mandatory multidisciplinary team evaluation is recommended for all patients with pleural mesothelioma [MS-2].
Histologic subtype (epithelioid vs. biphasic/sarcomatoid) is critical for prognosis and treatment planning, particularly regarding the role of surgery [9,96, MS-13].

Systemic therapy, specifically immunotherapy-based combinations, is now the preferred first-line treatment for most patients, regardless of surgical resectability, based on MARS and MARS2 trial results [187,188, MS-12, MS-13].
Nivolumab + ipilimumab is a preferred first-line option, especially for non-epithelioid histology where it showed superior OS compared to chemotherapy (HR 0.48) [9,149].
Pemetrexed-based chemotherapy has moved from 'Preferred' to 'Other Recommended' for subsequent therapy, while nivolumab ± ipilimumab is now preferred post-chemotherapy [PM-C].
Prophylactic radiotherapy to prevent procedure-tract metastases is not routinely recommended based on the SMART trial [230, PM-D].

Pleural IMRT after P/D can improve survival but should only be performed at experienced centers due to the high risk of fatal pneumonitis if lung dose constraints are not strictly met [194,195, PM-D].
Patients with germline BAP1 mutations have a better prognosis and require genetic counseling and surveillance for other BAP1-associated cancers [41,42, MS-3].
